Forced level failure if not completed in time

danger_signals.meaning: Major threat encounter requiring boss-level combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual effect around corrupted castle lords
danger_signals.meaning: Alien corruption / possession of daimyō authority

Scrolls, fireballs, lightning explosives, cloaks scattered in environment

safety_signals.meaning: Opportunity to gain extra lives through skill
Temporary invulnerability to enemies and hazards

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Imperial/shogunate insignia (implied through Takamaru's samurai status)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle structures, samurai presence as player-aligned forces

faction_indicators.color_identity: Color-coded castles: Aosame (blue), Akasame (red), Ryokusame (green), Momosame (peach/pink)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Evil sphere of power held by each lord
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each castle lord possesses distinct visual appearance reflecting their color theme
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Four distinct castle locations with thematic environmental design matching color identity

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Murasame stone statue animated by alien creature
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Alien entity revealed within stone statue form
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Murasame Castle (final location), supernatural aura effects

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Generic ninja and hannya creature designs
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy types: samurai, ninja, hannya (demon mask appearance)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Summoned by evil spheres; present throughout all four castles

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Implied through narrative; Murasame Castle housed peaceful stone statue Murasame
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Villagers lived peacefully; no enemy activity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Golden shining object falls from sky onto castle; deafening visual/audio effect
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Stone statue animates; deathly shrieks; alien entity takes control

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Four neighboring castles display corruption through environmental design; each lord surrounded by evil sphere aura
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Daimyō lords summon ninja armies and monsters; villagers attacked; castle chambers filled with enemies

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Takamaru appears in castle environments; enemy density present; treasure/power-up availability
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies actively patrol and attack; bosses engage in combat; level timer indicates urgency

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Completion of castle section transitions to next location; boss enemy removed from chamber
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Reduced enemy threat in that castle; progression toward Murasame Castle unlocked

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Murasame Castle chamber with full alien entity revealed; maximum visual complexity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final boss battle; alien entity demonstrates most dangerous attack patterns

environmental_storytelling.example: Four castles named by color (Aosame=Blue, Akasame=Red, Ryokusame=Green, Momosame=Peach) signal distinct regions and daimyō lords; visual progression through color sequence conveys advancing difficulty and territorial control
Each corrupted daimyō lord visually displays their respective evil sphere of power; the sphere is the visual anchor for the corruption narrative across all four castles
Samurai, ninja, and hannya (demon mask) enemy appearances draw from Japanese cultural iconography; hannya in particular signals supernatural/demonic corruption beyond normal human threat
Each level divided into two parts (path to castle, castle interior); innermost chambers house castle lords; visual architecture conveys increasing sanctity and power concentration toward the core
Golden shining object falling from sky introduces visual aesthetic foreign to Edo-period Japan setting; this visual disconnect communicates otherworldly threat and rupture of normal world order
Rescue-able princesses in castles provide civilian presence; their sprites signal stakes beyond mere combat; rescuing them grants life rewards, tying mechanical progression to narrative morality

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Attacks with katana at close range, shurikens at distance; deflects projectiles with katana
Becomes invisible and invulnerable; moves freely through enemy positions without taking damage
Cannot be damaged by any enemy attack or hazard
Summon ninja armies and monsters; initiate boss-level combat encounters; display aggressive attack patterns
Attack Takamaru on sight; vary attack patterns and movement based on enemy type (ninja vs samurai vs hannya)
Animates inanimate statue; extends control across four neighboring castles; initiates final confrontation at Murasame Castle
Supernatural enemy type; signals heightened supernatural threat beyond mortal samurai/ninja

notes: Nazo no Murasamejō communicates world-state primarily through color-coded castle environments and enemy type variation. The four-castle structure mirrors the four-lord corruption pattern, with visual progression signaling advancing narrative stakes. The alien invasion is marked by visual incongruity (golden shining object) against the Edo-period Japanese aesthetic, making the threat's foreignness immediately apparent. Enemy behavioral variety (samurai vs ninja vs hannya) encodes escalating supernatural corruption. Power-ups and princess rescue mechanics are spatially distributed to guide exploration. The game uses visual density (enemy count, projectile intensity) as a danger signal. No explicit faction color schemes are documented beyond the castle color names, so faction visual identity relies on spatial positioning and environmental design rather than banner/costume systems.
